questions.question: A network administrator wants employees to authenticate to the company's wireless network using their individual corporate usernames and passwords — NOT a shared passphrase. Which technology makes this possible?

answers.A: WPA3
answers.B: PSK (Pre-Shared Key)
answers.C: MFA

questions.explanation: 802.1X uses a centralized authentication server (like RADIUS or LDAP) so each user authenticates with their own corporate credentials. PSK uses a single shared passphrase for everyone. MFA adds additional factors but doesn't itself enable per-user credentials. WPA3 is an encryption standard, not a per-user authentication mechanism.

questions.question: 802.1X network authentication references three roles. Match the roles: the device trying to connect, the network switch or AP enforcing the policy, and the back-end database validating credentials. What is the CORRECT set of terms?

answers.A: Client / Server / Database
answers.B: Supplicant / Authenticator / Authentication Server
answers.C: User / Firewall / LDAP
answers.D: Host / Switch / RADIUS only

questions.explanation: In 802.1X, the SUPPLICANT is the device requesting access, the AUTHENTICATOR is the switch or AP enforcing policy, and the AUTHENTICATION SERVER (often RADIUS backed by LDAP/Active Directory) validates the credentials. Knowing this vocabulary is key for exam questions.

questions.question: Which of the following security concerns is MOST commonly associated with a hybrid cloud environment?

answers.A: Lack of containerization support
answers.B: IoT device compatibility
answers.C: Network protection mismatches between cloud providers
answers.D: Insufficient physical data center security

questions.explanation: Hybrid clouds span multiple private and public cloud environments. The added complexity makes it easy to inadvertently apply inconsistent authentication, permissions, and security controls across providers — creating network protection mismatches. Containerization and IoT issues are not specifically tied to hybrid cloud.

questions.question: A security log shows 445+ failed SSH login attempts against a single account from the same IP address in rapid succession. Which attack does this BEST describe?

answers.A: Password spraying
answers.B: Downgrade attack
answers.C: Brute force
answers.D: Credential stuffing

questions.explanation: BRUTE FORCE tries a massive number of combinations against one account until the password is found — the 445 attempts are a dead giveaway. PASSWORD SPRAYING tries a small number of common passwords across MANY accounts to stay under lockout thresholds. Spraying avoids alerts; brute force does not.

questions.question: An attacker tries the password 'Summer2024!' against 500 different user accounts, then tries 'Welcome1!' against those same 500 accounts. This is designed to avoid account lockout triggers. What attack is this?

answers.A: Brute force
answers.B: Password spraying
answers.C: Credential stuffing
answers.D: Rainbow table

questions.explanation: PASSWORD SPRAYING tries a few very common passwords across a large number of accounts to stay under lockout thresholds. Brute force exhaustively tries many passwords against one account. Credential stuffing uses stolen username/password pairs from breaches.

questions.question: A company is rolling out mobile devices to field staff. The company needs to own and manage the devices but still allow employees to use them for personal purposes. Which deployment model applies?

answers.A: BYOD
answers.B: CYOD
answers.C: COPE
answers.D: MDM

questions.explanation: COPE (Corporate-Owned, Personally Enabled) means the COMPANY owns and manages the device (solving MDM standardization issues) but employees may also use it for personal use. BYOD = employee owns it. CYOD = company buys it but employee chooses the model. MDM is a management platform, not a deployment model.

questions.question: A company policy REQUIRES individuals to use their personal phones for work tasks. The company does not purchase any devices. Which mobile deployment model is this?

answers.A: CYOD
answers.B: MDM
answers.C: COPE
answers.D: BYOD

questions.explanation: BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) is when employees use their personally-owned devices for work. The company requires the personal phone — it does not provide one. CYOD = company buys, employee chooses. COPE = company owns but allows personal use.

questions.question: An organization maintains a large customer database. Who is responsible for managing the ACCESS RIGHTS and applying day-to-day security controls to that data?

answers.A: Data processor
answers.B: Data owner
answers.C: Data subject
answers.D: Data custodian

questions.explanation: The DATA CUSTODIAN handles the operational management of data — setting permissions, applying security controls, and maintaining access rights. The DATA OWNER is typically a senior executive who makes high-level business decisions about the data. The DATA SUBJECT is the individual the data is ABOUT (e.g., a customer). The DATA PROCESSOR handles data under instructions from the controller.

questions.question: Customer payment and shipping records are being stored and protected for three years. The CUSTOMERS whose addresses and payment details are stored are called what in data privacy terminology?

answers.A: Data controller
answers.B: Data owner
answers.C: Data subject
answers.D: Data processor

questions.explanation: A DATA SUBJECT is the individual whose personal data is being processed. Customers providing their payment info and addresses are the data subjects. The DATA CONTROLLER determines the purpose and means of processing. The DATA PROCESSOR acts on the controller's instructions.

questions.question: A Certificate Authority (CA) digitally signs a website's certificate. What cryptographic purpose does this CA signature serve?

answers.A: It encrypts the certificate for confidentiality
answers.B: It compresses the certificate for transmission
answers.C: It adds trust to the certificate — if you trust the CA, you trust the certificate
answers.D: It creates a symmetric key for the TLS session

questions.explanation: A CA applies its DIGITAL SIGNATURE to a certificate to vouch for its authenticity. Clients that trust the CA will also trust certificates the CA has signed. This is the chain of trust in PKI. The CA signature does not encrypt the certificate or create session keys.

questions.question: An IPS is deployed at each warehouse of a shipping company to watch for suspicious traffic patterns. How should this security control BEST be categorized?

answers.A: Deterrent
answers.B: Compensating
answers.C: Directive
answers.D: Detective

questions.explanation: An IPS DETECTS attacks and can log/alert on them — making it a DETECTIVE control. It can also block known attacks (making it PREVENTIVE as well), but DETECTIVE is the best fit here since the question emphasizes watching and identifying suspicious patterns. A deterrent discourages action without detecting it.

questions.question: An organization cancels its ransomware insurance because premiums exceeded budget and decides to handle potential ransomware incidents internally. Which risk strategy does this represent?

answers.A: Risk mitigation
answers.B: Risk acceptance
answers.C: Risk transference
answers.D: Risk avoidance

questions.explanation: RISK ACCEPTANCE means the organization consciously decides to bear the risk itself rather than paying to transfer or reduce it. RISK TRANSFERENCE was the original insurance purchase — paying a third party to absorb the financial impact. Canceling insurance and handling it internally = accepting the risk.

questions.question: A company expects to lose approximately 8 laptops to theft each year, and each laptop costs $1,200 to replace. What is the ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy)?

answers.A: The ARO is $1,200
answers.B: The ALE is $1,200
answers.C: The ALE is $9,600
answers.D: The ARO is $9,600

questions.explanation: ALE = SLE × ARO. SLE (Single Loss Expectancy) = $1,200 per laptop. ARO (Annualized Rate of Occurrence) = 8 laptops/year. ALE = $1,200 × 8 = $9,600. The ARO is the COUNT (8 units); the SLE is the cost of ONE event; the ALE is the total yearly expected loss.

questions.question: Which metric BEST describes the estimated NUMBER OF TIMES a specific loss event (like laptop theft) is expected to occur in a single year?

answers.A: ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy)
answers.B: SLE (Single Loss Expectancy)
answers.C: ARO (Annualized Rate of Occurrence)
answers.D: MTTR (Mean Time to Repair)

questions.explanation: ARO is the estimated number of times an event occurs per year (e.g., 7 stolen laptops per year → ARO = 7). ALE is the total yearly financial loss (SLE × ARO). SLE is the cost of a SINGLE occurrence.

questions.question: An employee is suspected of bypassing corporate internet security controls by tethering their laptop to a personal cellular hotspot. The laptop is NOT on the corporate network. Which TWO sources of evidence would be MOST useful to validate this?

answers.A: HIPS logs and host-based firewall logs
answers.B: UTM logs and next-generation firewall logs
answers.C: Web application firewall events and UTM logs
answers.D: SIEM alerts and proxy server logs

questions.explanation: When a device is NOT on the corporate network, corporate perimeter tools (UTM, NGFW, WAF, proxy) see none of its traffic. The only sources of evidence are tools ON THE DEVICE ITSELF — a HIPS (Host-based Intrusion Prevention System) and host-based firewall logs will capture what traffic the laptop was generating regardless of which network it used.

questions.question: A penetration test engagement provides the testing team with only the external IP addresses of the target servers — no internal diagrams, no credentials, no source code. Which test environment type is this?

answers.A: White box (full knowledge)
answers.B: Active reconnaissance
answers.C: Partially known environment (gray box)
answers.D: Defensive engagement

questions.explanation: A PARTIALLY KNOWN (gray box) environment provides LIMITED information — in this case, just IP addresses. White box provides full knowledge (architecture, source code, credentials). Black box provides nothing. The IP addresses were given by the client, so the team is not performing active reconnaissance to discover them.

questions.question: A company's authentication system uses security clearance LABELS assigned to every file and folder (e.g., 'confidential', 'secret'). Users are assigned a MAXIMUM clearance level and can only access objects at or below their level. What access control model is this?

answers.A: Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
answers.B: Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
answers.C: Rule-based Access Control
answers.D: Role-based Access Control (RBAC)

questions.explanation: MAC assigns security labels to objects and clearance levels to users — access is determined by the SYSTEM based on labels, not by the resource owner. DAC lets resource owners decide who gets access. RBAC assigns permissions based on job role. Rule-based uses system-enforced rules like time-of-day restrictions.

questions.question: Which access control model allows the RESOURCE OWNER to grant or restrict access to their own files and folders at their own discretion?

answers.A: Mandatory Access Control (MAC)
answers.B: Role-based Access Control (RBAC)
answers.C: Discretionary Access Control (DAC)
answers.D: Rule-based Access Control

questions.explanation: DAC puts the access decision in the hands of the RESOURCE OWNER. Traditional file system permissions (where the file creator can share with whoever they choose) are a DAC example. In MAC, access is enforced by the system regardless of owner preference.

questions.question: A security administrator needs to provide authentication for 802.1X network access control. Which protocol would be the BEST back-end choice for validating usernames and passwords?

answers.A: SIEM
answers.B: LDAP
answers.C: SPF
answers.D: WAF

questions.explanation: LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is a standard protocol for centralized authentication — it's the directory service (like Active Directory) that stores usernames and passwords. 802.1X uses a RADIUS server as the intermediary, which then queries LDAP/AD. SIEM is for log analysis; WAF is a web application firewall; SPF is an email standard.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST provides FEDERATION for authentication?

answers.A: SSO — allowing one login to access all internal corporate resources
answers.B: Federation — allowing users to authenticate to one organization's resources using credentials from a DIFFERENT, trusted organization
answers.C: 802.1X — requiring corporate credentials for network access
answers.D: LDAP — providing a central directory for all user accounts

questions.explanation: FEDERATION enables cross-organization authentication — your credentials from Company A let you log into Company B's resources. SSO is about one login granting access to many resources WITHIN the same organization. A website that lets you 'Sign in with Google' is an example of federation.

questions.question: A company deploys applications across multiple cloud providers and needs efficient, dynamic connectivity between users and all those cloud services. Which connection type is BEST suited?

answers.A: Air-gapped network
answers.C: Site-to-site IPsec VPN
answers.D: SD-WAN

questions.explanation: SD-WAN (Software-Defined Wide Area Network) is purpose-built for efficiently connecting users to cloud-based applications across multiple providers. Site-to-site IPsec VPN is static and difficult to scale as applications move between cloud providers. SD-WAN adapts dynamically and manages multi-cloud connectivity well.

questions.question: Two applications in a data center must NEVER communicate with each other under any circumstances. Which solution provides the STRONGEST guarantee of this isolation?

answers.A: Firewall rules between the applications
answers.B: Separate VLANs
answers.C: Air gap (physical separation)
answers.D: SDN (Software-Defined Networking)

questions.explanation: An AIR GAP is a physical separation — no network connection of any kind exists between the two systems. This is the strongest isolation. Firewalls and VLANs are logical controls that could be misconfigured. SDN provides network control abstraction but not isolation. If systems must NEVER communicate, physical air-gapping is the only absolute guarantee.

questions.question: During an incident, an administrator disconnects an infected server's ethernet cable, disables an unknown user account, and configures a firewall rule to block outbound file transfers. Which incident response phase does this BEST represent?

answers.A: Eradication
answers.B: Containment
answers.C: Lessons learned
answers.D: Recovery

questions.explanation: CONTAINMENT stops the bleeding — preventing the incident from spreading further without yet removing the cause. Disconnecting the cable and blocking the transfer are containment actions. ERADICATION comes AFTER containment and involves removing the malware and patching vulnerabilities. RECOVERY is rebuilding and restoring systems to normal.

questions.question: An incident response team wants to walk through their disaster recovery plans and discuss procedures in a conference room WITHOUT touching or modifying any live systems. Which exercise type fits this requirement?

answers.A: Hot site fail-over test
answers.B: Full simulation
answers.C: Tabletop exercise
answers.D: Penetration test

questions.explanation: A TABLETOP EXERCISE is a discussion-based walk-through — no systems are touched, no changes are made. The team discusses 'what would we do if...' scenarios. A simulation involves actually executing some procedures. A hot site fail-over requires cutting over to the DR site. Only the tabletop guarantees zero infrastructure changes.
